notes.note: The haruspices --- the corps of Etruscan diviners maintained at Rome to interpret prodigies (lightning, monstrous births, ground-rumblings, etc.) and prescribe expiations. Their craft was the Etrusca disciplina, distinct from the Roman college of pontiffs and the augurs.
Cn. Cornelius Lentulus Marcellinus, consul 56 BC; the optimate consul under whose presidency Cicero in this year recovers his political standing in the Senate.

notes.anchor: the house of the pontifex maximus
notes.note: The Bona Dea outrage of December 62 BC: Clodius, in woman's dress, infiltrated the rites celebrated for the Roman people by the Vestal Virgins at the house of the pontifex maximus (Caesar's house, his wife Pompeia presiding). The trial of 61 BC, in which Clodius was acquitted by a bribed jury, is the foundational episode of Cicero-vs-Clodius.

T. Annius Milo, tribune of the plebs 57 BC and (by the time of this speech) the optimate counterweight to Clodius. The pending prosecution of Clodius is here promised to him as ``a victim already vowed.''

Strepitus cum fremitu: the prodigy in the agro Latiniensi --- a noise from the ground south of Rome --- which the haruspices had read as a warning that polluted games and other faults required expiation. The haruspical text is read out across sections 20-60.

Cicero lists the twenty-strong attendance at the pontifical hearing of late September 57 BC that freed his Palatine site from the consecration Clodius had imposed; the parallel set-piece is De Domo Sua. The list includes the consul P. Lentulus, P. Servilius, M. Lucullus, Q. Metellus Scipio, M. Lepidus, M. Crassus, the rex sacrorum L. Claudius, the flamen Martialis L. Lentulus, and the flamen Quirinalis Sex. Caesar.

P. Valerius Publicola, the consul of 509 BC, the legendary first consul after the expulsion of the kings; given a public house on the Velia for his services. Cicero compares the standing of his own restored house favourably.
An expiatory offering owed (postilio) when a portent has indicated divine displeasure; the haruspical text identifies the gods to whom postilia are due.

The annual festival (4-10 April) of Cybele, the Magna Mater Idaea, brought to Rome from Pessinus in 204 BC during the second Punic war. Performed on the Palatine before the goddess's temple; the games featured drama and chariot-races. The Magna Mater is the rumbling figure walking through fields and groves.

The two best-known slave-revolt leaders: Athenion of the Sicilian slave war of 104-100 BC, Spartacus of the gladiatorial revolt of 73-71 BC. Cicero's gibe is that Clodius's slave-loosed Megalesia was the kind of Megalesia those would have given, not the kind a Claudius would.

P. Cornelius Scipio Nasica, judged the optimus vir of the Roman people and chosen to receive the Magna Mater stone when it arrived from Pessinus in 204 BC. Q. Claudia, the great-aunt of Clodius's branch, was the chastest of Roman matrons and pulled the stone to its place when the boat had stuck on the Tiber.
Brogitarus, a Galatian dynast and son-in-law of Deiotarus, who as tribune Clodius caused to be named king and granted the priesthood of Pessinus by a clause in his legislation, in exchange for cash.

Deiotarus, tetrarch (and later king by senatorial decree) of the Tolistobogii in Galatia, a faithful client of Rome through the Mithridatic and Pompeian wars. Cicero defends him here for refusing to pay Clodius and for recovering Pessinus.

Q. Seius Postumus, an equestrian whose house was acquired by Clodius after Seius's murder; Cicero claims the murdered man's house contained a chapel and altars, making it religiously polluted in the way Clodius wished to claim of Cicero's house.

Philoctetes (the suffering hero of Sophocles's lost or surviving play, with the wound from the snake-bite) and Athamas (the matricide-king driven mad by Hera) --- the two stock theatrical figures for, respectively, bodily and mental punishment by the gods. Cicero argues the worse fate is the second.

The most concentrated catalogue of the Bona Dea outrage's symbolic detail: the crocota (saffron robe), the mitra (Lydian or eastern headband), women's slippers, purple ribbons, the strophium (breast-band), the psalterium (lyre, a feminine accompaniment) --- the whole female-cultic kit in which Clodius infiltrated the rites.

notes.anchor: the dagger in the temple of Castor
notes.note: The dagger discovered on a man at Pompey's audience in the temple of Castor in early 58 BC, which led to Pompey's withdrawal from public business and his confinement to his house for the rest of Clodius's tribunate.

Two technical terms (deteriores, repulsi) of the Etrusca disciplina for the kinds of citizens whose advancement to office is to be guarded against. Cicero glosses repulsi as men recently rejected by the voters for gladiatorial-show bribery, and deteriores as Clodius and his herd.

A iustitium (literally ``stand-still of justice'') is the formal suspension of all public business --- courts closed, treasury closed, jurisdiction halted --- declared in the gravest civic emergencies (foreign invasion, sedition). Clodius had floated the idea in a contio; Cicero treats it as a coup-program.

The reference is obscure; possibly to Clodius's elder brother Appius, adopted by a Fonteius, or to Clodius's own claimed adoption history (his transitus ad plebem in 59 BC was carried by a P. Fonteius). The general charge is that Clodius has buried the Claudian name.

The consensus ordinum --- the union of senate and equestrian order --- was Cicero's signature programmatic formula throughout the post-consulship years; its breaking is here entered as one of the symptoms of the late Republic, alongside the empty treasury, the publicans' lost contracts, and the votes held by a few.
